Software delivery.P1: Delivers (with guidance) small to medium-sized user stories on time and with quality. Technical impact is at the small to medium-sized user story level within a team (e.g. contributing code to an existing design). When requested, helps their teammates overcome obstacles, resolve blockers, and complete work tasks to ensure overall team progress.
Software delivery.P2: Independently delivers sprint-level user stories on time and with quality. Technical impact is at the larger user story level within a team (e.g. building new functionality within a service). When requested, helps their teammates overcome obstacles, resolve blockers, and complete work tasks to ensure overall team progress.
Software delivery.P3: Delivers large-sized (up to a full quarter) features or a series of user stories on time and with quality. Can serve as a feature execution lead. Technical impact is at the feature or component level within a single team. (e.g. defining and building a component or a feature). Starts to proactively help their teammates overcome obstacles, resolve blockers, complete work tasks to ensure overall team progress.
Software delivery.P4: Delivers single to multiple quarter projects within a team on time and with quality. E.g. a master feature that consists of multiple features. Can serve as a master feature execution lead. Technical impact is at the master feature or service level within a single team. (e.g. defining and building a service or a master feature). Consistently helps their teammates overcome obstacles, resolve blockers, and complete work tasks to ensure overall team progress.
Software delivery.P5: Delivers multi-quarter projects that span multiple teams on time and with quality. E.g. an initiative that consists of multiple master features. Technical impact spans across multiple teams in the organization or depth of impact significantly advances key systems in the organization. Consistently works across teams to help them overcome obstacles, resolve blockers, and support each other.
Software delivery.P6: Delivers multi-quarter projects that span teams from multiple organizations on time and with quality. Technical impact spans multiple engineering organizations and/or depth of impact significantly advances mission-critical systems for the organization. Consistently works across the engineering organization to enable teams to support each other.

Coding craft.P1: Writes code that often needs oversight from more senior engineers with applications of coding best practices. Requests feedback via code reviews. Demonstrates ability to learn from feedback to improve craft. Diligently follows release processes/steps for products & services (e.g. has performed routine production deployments according to the runbook).
Coding craft.P2: Writes code that may need oversight from more senior engineers with applications of coding best practices. Possesses basic code review skills. Starts to influence existing release processes for products & services (e.g. proposes enhancements).
Coding craft.P3: Writes code that rarely needs modification based on code review. Provides thoughtful and actionable code review feedback. Influences standards and checklists. Defines and automates release processes for products & services (defines or enhances runbook).
Coding craft.P4: Writes code, held up as an example for others. Defines all aspects of code review standards, including code quality, and drives adoption across teams, including doing code reviews. Exemplary release processes for the most complicated product releases and service migrations (e.g. complex platform migrations).
Coding craft.P5: Writes code, held up as an example for others. Defines all aspects of code review standards, including code quality, and drives adoption across teams, including doing code reviews. Exemplary release processes for the most complicated product releases and service migrations (e.g. complex platform migrations).
Coding craft.P6: Writes code, held up as an example for others. Defines all aspects of code review standards, including code quality, and drives adoption across teams, including doing code reviews. Exemplary release processes for the most complicated product releases and service migrations (e.g. complex platform migrations).

Technical design.P1: Actively participates in design reviews and in the designs of the features they're executing on. Solves problems that are straightforward and well-defined. Executes on technical requirements that are already defined.
Technical design.P2: Influences the designs of the features their pod is executing on. Owns simple designs (e.g. REST API endpoint). Solves problems that are of medium complexity and well-defined. Executes on technical requirements that are defined on a high level, but some details might be missing.
Technical design.P3: Owns designing solutions to moderately complex projects at the feature or component level. Solves problems that are well-defined, but complex. Executes on technical requirements that are not defined, but business requirements are. Independently prioritizes and triages requirements.
Technical design.P4: Owns designs for services within a product area. Solves complex problems while avoiding unnecessary complexity. Is a reviewer of moderately complex system designs within an engineering organization. Given business goals, drives clarity on technical requirements and technical direction.
Technical design.P5: Drives long-term designs for multiple services, frequently taking a hands-on role during implementation. Is a reviewer of designs across an engineering organization. Solves problems that are complex and/or ambiguous. Given business goals, derives goals and execution plans.
Technical design.P6: Drives the technical vision and long-term designs across a product area, occasionally taking a hands-on role during implementation. Reviews designs across multiple engineering organizations. Solves problems that are the most difficult presented to the engineering organization. Identifies and resolves problems that are hidden between layers of business and technology, anticipating their future impact.

Operations.P1: Participates in team-level Slack interrupt rotation. Solves production issues by following the team's operational runbooks. Starts logging or capturing operational metrics for individual features.
Operations.P2: Participates in pod-level Slack interrupt and on-call rotation. Independently solves simple production issues. Captures operational metrics for individual features and starts creating usable metrics. Owns postmortem action items.
Operations.P3: Trusted on-call/support engineer who is able to represent the team and resolve moderate complexity issues autonomously. Drives team improvement in operational maturity on the feature level. E.g. owns postmortems for incidents in their team.
Operations.P4: Expert on-call/support engineer from their area. Solves complex issues that require a deep understanding of the system's inner workings. Drives team improvement in operational maturity at the team level. Bar-raising postmortem owner. Reviews/audits postmortems. Applies learning from other teams. Prevents recurrence of similar failures.
Operations.P5: Leads troubleshooting of any problem in production systems from their organization, identifies owners and helps drive resolution. Drives generalizable improvements in operational maturity for an engineering organization (e.g. in monitoring or alerting).
Operations.P6: Leads troubleshooting of any problem in production systems, identifies owners and helps drive resolution. Holds multiple engineering organizations to the highest operational standards (e.g. reliability, security, regulatory compliance).

Leadership and Communication.P1: Participates in the team's ceremonies. Onboarding for engineering interns and new hires. Makes others feel comfortable and welcome. Openly shares opinions in a respectful way. Consistently available in person based on in-office expectations. Contributes to building a feedback culture by seeking and providing peer feedback.
Leadership and Communication.P2: Provides constructive feedback in retrospectives. Actively mentors engineers P2 or below. Communicates with clarity and conciseness. Openly shares opinions and addresses others' concerns without blame. Same as P1 for in-office and feedback culture.
Leadership and Communication.P3: Starts to influence the team's processes and propose improvements. Actively mentors engineers P3 or below. Proactively includes new and unfamiliar people. Can tactfully resolve differences of opinion. Demonstrates an ability to help a group of people arrive at a consensus even when there were opposing views. Same as P1 for in-office and feedback culture.
Leadership and Communication.P4: Identifies gaps in the team's processes and implements changes to improve them or leads a team as their L1. Actively mentors engineers P4 or below. Holds team members accountable in a clear and kind way. Fosters a culture where teammates are encouraged to openly share opinions. Same as P1 for in-office and feedback culture.
Leadership and Communication.P5: Drives collaboration across multiple teams that are delivering towards a single goal. Actively mentors engineers P5 or below. Helps turn an unhealthy team into one that is productive and empathetic. Fosters a culture across teams where people are encouraged to share their opinion and negotiates disparate viewpoints to find common ground. Same as P1 for in-office and feedback culture.
Leadership and Communication.P6: Drives collaboration across multiple organizations that are delivering towards a single goal. Actively mentors engineers P6 or below. Helps turn around an unhealthy organization (e.g. culture of politics and fear). People across the organization seek guidance from this person. Same as P1 for in-office and feedback culture.
